result:
I want to write about the quiet magic of in-between spaces.

We spend our lives aiming for destinations—the finished project, the arrival at the vacation, the summit of the mountain, the achievement of the goal. Our culture is a cathedral built to the gods of completion and arrival. The to-do list is our scripture, the checked box our sacrament. But I have come to suspect, more and more, that the real texture of life, its most vivid and poignant dye, is found not in the arrivals but in the transit halls. Not in the summit, but in the tree-shrouded, oxygen-thin path just below it. Not in the finished novel, but in the daily, hesitant communion with the blinking cursor. These are the in-between spaces. The liminal zones.

Think of the physical ones first, the ones we’ve all felt. The airport at 3 a.m., a capsule of hushed light floating in a sea of darkness and runway strobes. It belongs to no city, no country, really. It is a parenthesis. Everyone there is suspended between a “was” and a “will be.” Strangers share a profound, unspoken fellowship of transition. There’s a melancholy, but also a strange freedom. In the in-between, your old identity is temporarily invalid, and your new one hasn’t yet been stamped. You are anonymous, weightless. Similarly, the hotel room. It is meticulously neutral, a stage set with no play. It holds the ghost-impressions of a thousand other stories, other bodies, other dreams, all washed clean with chemical citrus. You are both nowhere and everywhere in a hotel room. It is a sanctuary for a self that exists only for a night.

Then there are the temporal in-betweens. The half-hour before a guest arrives, when the house is clean and quiet, charged with a peaceful expectancy. The week between Christmas and New Year’s—that glorious, timeless bubble where days of the week lose all meaning, where you can eat chocolate for breakfast and no one, not even you, seems to mind. Work is a ghost town, and time itself feels flabby and soft, a gift of pure, unallocated potential. It’s a week for staring out windows at bare trees, for reading novels in a single sitting, for feeling the slow turn of the year in your bones.

But the most profound in-between spaces are interior. They are the emotional and psychological thresholds. The period after a great sadness has begun to soften but before any new joy has taken root. It’s not depression; it’s a quiet, grey, fertile fallow field. You feel tender, but open. The world seems to have a finer grain. You notice the way dust motes dance in a sunbeam, not with joy, but with a deep, quiet curiosity. This is the in-between of healing, where you are no longer defined by the wound, but not yet defined by whatever comes next. It is a sacred, if fragile, state of becoming.

Or the creative in-between: that foggy, intuitive space between having an idea and executing it. It’s the most thrilling and terrifying place for an artist. The idea is perfect, shimmering, whole in your mind. The execution will be clumsy, flawed, earthly. The magic is in the dwelling, in the composting of thought and feeling and reference, in the walks where you’re not quite thinking of it, but it’s simmering on some back burner of your soul. To rush out of this space is to kill the butterfly in the chrysalis. You must learn to love the murk, the not-knowing, the ambiguous shapes in the mental fog.

We are taught to fear these spaces. We call them boredom, indecision, purgatory. We fill them with podcasts and scrolling, with frantic activity and noise, lest we have to feel their unsettling quiet. We want answers, conclusions, closure. But the in-between is the realm of questions. And questions are so much more interesting, more spacious, than answers. An answer is a door closing. A question is a horizon expanding.

In nature, the most potent in-betweens are the thresholds of day: dawn and dusk. The French call it *l’heure bleue*, the blue hour. The Spaniards have *la hora mágica*, the magic hour. It is a time of transformation, when the world is neither day nor night, but something else entirely. Shapes blur. Colors bleed. The familiar view from your window becomes a Monet painting. It is a daily reminder that transition is not an ugly interruption of stable states, but a beautiful, ephemeral state in itself. The dragonfly exists in the in-between of water and air. The tadpole in the in-between of fish and frog. These are not errors or failed states; they are the essential, miraculous process of being.

Perhaps learning to appreciate the in-between is learning to love the verb over the noun. We are human *beings*, but we are obsessed with the static quality of *being*—being successful, being happy, being finished. We forget the primal, pulsing truth of *becoming*. Life is not a collection of destinations reached. It is a current, a flow, a continuous, shimmering process of change. The in-between spaces are where that becoming is most palpable, most pure. They are the connective tissue of a life, the marrow where meaning is produced, often silently and unseen.

So I am trying to cultivate a reverence for the wait. For the train delay that grants ten unexpected minutes of people-watching. For the illness that forces a week of horizontal stillness, a return to the elemental rhythms of breath and soup and sleep. For the unresolved argument that sits between us, demanding a more thoughtful, slower kind of listening. For the dream you haven’t yet dared to start, which exists in its most perfect, potent form in the kingdom of possibility.

The next time you find yourself in an in-between—stuck in traffic, in a waiting room, in the silent pause after a question hangs in the air, in the restless hours before a life change—instead of reaching for your phone to blot it out, try to sink into it. Look out the window. Feel the quality of the air. Listen to the hum of the fluorescent lights or the distant sound of rain. You are in a portal. Nothing is demanded of you here. You are, for this fleeting moment, free from the tyranny of the previous story and the burden of the next one. You are simply *here*, in the secret, humming heart of transition itself. And that might just be the most real place of all.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
